This is likely to satisfy the fundamental dishonesty test. If found, QOCS protection is disapplied in full and the defendant can enforce a full costs order. Under s57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015 the court must also dismiss the claim unless doing so would be substantially unjust.
QOCS.summary_line: QOCS · FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY · CPR 44.16(1) · CENTRAL TO CLAIM · SURVEILLANCE FOOTAGE · DISAPPLY IN FULL · s57 CJA 2015 DISMISS CLAIM

QOCS.front: Going deeper: Has qualified one-way costs shifting (QOCS) adequately replaced recoverability of success fees and ATE premiums for PI claimants?
QOCS.answer:
The Jackson reforms (LASPO 2012) abolished recoverability of success fees and ATE premiums in PI cases, replacing them with QOCS. Lord Justice Jackson argued QOCS would protect claimants from adverse costs while removing recoverable success fees and ATE premiums would reduce litigation costs overall.

The Access to Justice Foundation and Bar Council raised concerns that QOCS does not replicate the protection of a fully-funded ATE policy — in particular, it does not cover disbursements (court fees, expert fees, counsel's fees) in the event of loss. A claimant who loses a substantial PI claim may be left with significant disbursement liability even with QOCS protection.

Professor Rachael Mulheron (Civil Justice Quarterly, 2015) identified structural problems: the fundamental dishonesty exception (s57 Criminal Justice and Courts Act 2015) is used aggressively by insurers even in cases involving modest exaggeration, threatening to undermine QOCS protection for genuine claimants with peripheral dishonesty issues.

The interaction between CPR 44.16 and CPR 36.17 (QOCS and Part 36 set-off) has generated further uncertainty in recent case law.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers QOCS operation and the fundamental dishonesty exception; the reform debate is beyond the syllabus.
QOCS.summary_line: QOCS · JACKSON REFORMS · LASPO 2012 · SUCCESS FEES AND ATE REMOVED · DISBURSEMENTS NOT COVERED · FUNDAMENTAL DISHONESTY USED AGGRESSIVELY · MULHERON CRITIQUE

QOCS.front:
⚠️ TRAP CARD

A personal injury claimant wins on liability but is awarded only £8,000 in damages — less than the defendant's Part 36 offer of £12,000. The defendant has a costs order against the claimant from the date the relevant period expired. The claimant's solicitor says QOCS means the defendant cannot enforce any costs at all.
QOCS.issue: Students know QOCS protects PI claimants from adverse costs orders and apply it absolutely: 'QOCS means no costs liability'. The claimant won — liability established and damages awarded — which makes the QOCS protection feel even stronger.
QOCS.rule: QOCS does not provide absolute protection. Under CPR 44.14, orders for costs against a claimant may be enforced to the extent of the claimant's damages and interest awarded. The defendant's costs order (from expiry of the Part 36 relevant period) may be set off against the £8,000 award. If the defendant's costs exceed £8,000, the defendant can enforce up to £8,000 — the amount of the claimant's damages. The claimant ends up with nothing, not because QOCS was 'lost', but because CPR 44.14 permits enforcement up to the value of damages.
QOCS.application: The red herring is the label 'QOCS protection', which sounds absolute. Students know QOCS shields PI claimants from adverse costs orders and assume total immunity. But CPR 44.14 caps, not eliminates, liability — enforcement is limited to the amount of damages and interest awarded. Conduct that constitutes fundamental dishonesty removes QOCS entirely.
QOCS.conclusion: QOCS does not mean zero costs liability. It caps the claimant's costs liability at the level of their damages and interest — not at nil. Where a claimant fails to beat a Part 36 offer, the defendant's costs from expiry of the relevant period are set off against the claimant's damages. A claimant with a modest award against a large adverse costs order may recover nothing in practice.
QOCS.summary_line: QOCS · CPR 44.14 · ENFORCED UP TO LEVEL OF DAMAGES · NOT ABSOLUTE ZERO LIABILITY · PART 36 SET-OFF AGAINST DAMAGES · CLAIMANT MAY RECOVER NOTHING

Costs Budgeting.front:
When does costs budgeting apply, what must parties file, and what is the sanction for late filing?

⚠️ Link — Denton test (see FLK1-DR-057): the only route to escape the CPR 3.14 nil-budget sanction is a CPR 3.9 relief from sanctions application applying the Denton three-stage test. A one-day delay is sufficient to trigger the full sanction — the court does not scale it to the length of the delay.
Costs Budgeting.answer:
Costs budgeting applies to multi-track cases in the County Court and most High Court cases, except where the value exceeds £10 million or the case falls within specified exceptions (Commercial Court, Chancery Division specialist lists) (CPR 3.12).

Parties must file and exchange Precedent H costs budgets not later than 21 days before the first CMC/CCMC. The court approves the budget at the CCMC and the approved budget caps recoverable costs on detailed assessment, absent good reason to depart.

Sanction for late filing: if a party fails to file a budget on time, they are treated as having filed a budget comprising only court fees (CPR 3.14). This effectively limits recoverable costs to court fees only.

⚠️ The CPR 3.14 sanction is heavily tested. Late filing is not just penalised — it effectively destroys the party's costs recovery.
Costs Budgeting.summary_line: COSTS BUDGETING · MULTI-TRACK · PRECEDENT H · 21 DAYS BEFORE CMC · CPR 3.14 SANCTION · COURT FEES ONLY IF LATE

Costs Budgeting.front: Going deeper: Has the costs budgeting regime under CPR 3.12–3.18 improved or distorted the economics of civil litigation for parties of unequal means?
Costs Budgeting.answer:
The costs budgeting regime was introduced following Lord Justice Jackson's Review of Civil Litigation Costs (2010). Its purpose was to make litigation costs predictable and proportionate.

Supporters argue costs budgeting has reduced the 'chilling effect' of open-ended costs exposure, produced more realistic estimates, and enabled courts to manage the overall cost envelope more actively (Jackson LJ, extra-judicially, 2016).

Critics identify structural problems: (1) asymmetric impact — well-resourced parties are better placed to prepare thorough Precedent H budgets and to resist budget 'slashing' at CCMC; (2) the CPR 3.14 sanction (nil budget if late) operates as a sledgehammer regardless of the triviality of the delay; (3) budget-following costs under CPR 3.18 can produce outcomes where the receiving party recovers less than their actual costs even where those costs were reasonable and proportionate (Sharp v Blank [2017]); (4) budgeting front-loads costs — the CCMC itself can be expensive, disproportionately so in cases that settle early.

Academic commentary by Hodges, Vogenauer, and Peysner (Civil Justice Systems, OUP) identifies England and Wales as an outlier in the degree of judicial intervention in cost management compared to European systems.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers the basic costs budgeting rules; the reform debate is beyond the syllabus.
Costs Budgeting.summary_line: COSTS BUDGETING · JACKSON REFORMS · PRECEDENT H · ASYMMETRIC IMPACT · CPR 3.14 SLEDGEHAMMER · CPR 3.18 BUDGET-FOLLOWING · SHARP v BLANK [2017]

Assessment.front: What is the difference between summary assessment and detailed assessment of costs?
Assessment.answer:
Summary assessment: the court determines the amount of costs at the end of a hearing (usually an interim application or fast track trial). Each party must file a statement of costs (Form N260) at least 24 hours before the hearing. The court fixes the sum there and then.

Detailed assessment: a separate, later process used for larger or more complex costs disputes, and for all multi-track trials. The receiving party must commence detailed assessment proceedings by serving a bill of costs within 3 months of the costs order (CPR 47.7). A costs officer (district judge) determines the amount at a detailed assessment hearing.

Both processes apply the standard or indemnity basis as directed.
Assessment.summary_line: SUMMARY ASSESSMENT · N260 · 24 HOURS · AT HEARING · DETAILED ASSESSMENT · CPR 47 · BILL OF COSTS · 3 MONTHS · COSTS OFFICER

Assessment.front: Following a fast track trial, the winning claimant asks for summary assessment. The defendant argues complex costs issues require detailed assessment. Advise.
Assessment.answer: Summary assessment is the normal procedure at the end of a fast track trial. The court determines costs there and then without a separate detailed assessment process. Each party should have filed a statement of costs (Form N260) at least 24 hours before the hearing. The defendant's argument for detailed assessment is unlikely to succeed — detailed assessment is generally reserved for multi-track cases and complex costs disputes, not fast track trials. Some disputed items do not automatically require detailed assessment. If genuinely complex issues arise (e.g. disputes over success fees or ATE premiums), the court may direct those specific items to detailed assessment while summarily assessing the rest.
Assessment.summary_line: SUMMARY ASSESSMENT · FAST TRACK TRIAL NORM · N260 · 24 HOURS · JUDGE DECIDES THERE AND THEN · DETAILED ASSESSMENT FOR COMPLEX CASES

Litigants in Person.front: Can a litigant in person recover costs, and if so, at what rate?
Litigants in Person.answer:
A litigant in person may recover costs under CPR 46.5. They may claim: (1) costs for work done themselves, at two-thirds of the rate that would have been allowed had a legal representative done the work; (2) payments reasonably made for legal advice or assistance in connection with the proceedings; and (3) out-of-pocket expenses reasonably incurred.

For financial loss (e.g. time off work), the recoverable rate is subject to a cap under CPR 46.5(4)(b).

⚠️ Verify the current hourly financial loss cap against CPR PD 46 — figures are periodically updated and the SRA syllabus expects candidates to know the current limit. A litigant in person cannot recover a commercial rate for their own time if they have suffered no financial loss.
Litigants in Person.summary_line: LITIGANT IN PERSON · CPR 46.5 · TWO-THIRDS RATE · OWN WORK · LEGAL ADVICE COSTS · FINANCIAL LOSS CAP · VERIFY CURRENT FIGURE

Wasted Costs.front: What is a wasted costs order and what must be shown before one is made?
Wasted Costs.answer:
A wasted costs order (CPR 46.8, s51(6) Senior Courts Act 1981) may be made against a legal representative personally where costs have been incurred by a party as a result of the legal representative's improper, unreasonable, or negligent act or omission, and it is just in all the circumstances to make the order.

Three requirements must all be satisfied: (1) the conduct was improper, unreasonable, or negligent; (2) that conduct caused the wasted costs; (3) it is just to make the order. The legal representative must be given the opportunity to show cause before an order is made.

The power is exceptional — it is not a substitute for a professional negligence claim. Wasted costs orders are only available against legal representatives, not parties or third-party funders.

⚠️ All three requirements are cumulative. A negligent act that did not cause the costs claimed will not justify an order.
Wasted Costs.summary_line: WASTED COSTS · CPR 46.8 · s51(6) SCA 1981 · LEGAL REPRESENTATIVE PERSONALLY · THREE REQUIREMENTS: IMPROPER/UNREASONABLE/NEGLIGENT + CAUSED + JUST

Trial Procedure.front: What must a trial bundle contain and who is responsible for preparing it?
Trial Procedure.answer:
The trial bundle must contain (PD 39A): (1) the claim form and all statements of case; (2) all orders made; (3) any agreed statement of facts or chronology; (4) all witness statements served; (5) all expert reports and any joint statement; (6) all documents that any party intends to rely on or that are referred to in the above documents.

The claimant is responsible for preparing the bundle unless the court orders otherwise. It must be paginated and indexed. Filing deadlines: not less than 3 days before the hearing for fast track trials; not less than 7 days for multi-track trials.
Trial Procedure.summary_line: TRIAL BUNDLE · CLAIMANT PREPARES · PD 39A · PAGINATED AND INDEXED · 3 DAYS FAST TRACK · 7 DAYS MULTI-TRACK

Trial Procedure.front: What is the usual order of proceedings at a civil trial?
Trial Procedure.summary_line: TRIAL ORDER · CLAIMANT OPENS · CLAIMANT WITNESSES · DEFENDANT WITNESSES · EXPERTS · DEFENDANT CLOSES · CLAIMANT CLOSES LAST

Trial Procedure.front: In a fast track trial, the claimant's barrister proposes to give a 20-minute opening speech. The judge says this is unnecessary. Who is correct?
Trial Procedure.answer: The judge is correct. In fast track trials, opening speeches are frequently dispensed with by the judge's direction. Witness statements stand as examination-in-chief — the factual background is already before the court. A 20-minute opening in a one-day fast track trial is disproportionate. Under the overriding objective (CPR 1.1), the court must deal with cases at proportionate cost and expeditiously. The judge has the case management power to dispense with or limit opening speeches. The barrister may make brief introductory submissions but a substantive opening speech is not a right in a fast track case.
Trial Procedure.summary_line: FAST TRACK TRIAL · OPENING SPEECH DISPENSED WITH · OVERRIDING OBJECTIVE · JUDGE'S CASE MANAGEMENT POWERS · CLOSING SUBMISSIONS REMAIN

Burden & Standard of Proof.front: What is the standard of proof in civil proceedings, and does it vary with the seriousness of the allegation?
Burden & Standard of Proof.answer:
The standard of proof in civil proceedings is the balance of probabilities — a fact is proved if it is more likely than not to be true (i.e. more than 50% probable). This is lower than the criminal standard of beyond reasonable doubt.

The standard does not formally vary in civil proceedings — the House of Lords confirmed in Re B (Children) [2008] that there is a single civil standard regardless of the seriousness of the allegation (overruling the former idea of a sliding scale for grave allegations). However, in practice, the more serious the allegation, the stronger the evidence will need to be to establish it on the balance of probabilities, because inherently improbable events require more cogent evidence.

⚠️ A common exam trap is to apply a higher standard for allegations of fraud or serious misconduct in civil proceedings. The standard remains the balance of probabilities throughout.
Burden & Standard of Proof.summary_line: STANDARD OF PROOF · BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES · SINGLE CIVIL STANDARD · RE B [2008] · NO SLIDING SCALE · MORE PROBABLE THAN NOT

Burden & Standard of Proof.front: Who bears the burden of proof in civil proceedings, and in what circumstances does it reverse?
Burden & Standard of Proof.answer:
The general rule is that the party who asserts a fact bears the burden of proving it. In practice:
(1) The claimant bears the legal burden of proving every element of their cause of action on the balance of probabilities.
(2) The defendant bears the burden of proving any affirmative defence (e.g. contributory negligence, volenti, limitation defence, exemption clause reasonableness under UCTA 1977).
(3) The evidential burden (the burden of raising an issue with sufficient evidence to put it in play) may shift during trial even though the legal burden does not.

Specific reversals:
- CRA 2015 (consumer goods): during the first 6 months after delivery, the trader must prove the goods conformed to contract; after 6 months, the consumer must prove non-conformity.
- s33 LA 1980 applications: the claimant must positively establish that it would be equitable to disapply the limitation period.

⚠️ The evidential burden shifting during cross-examination does not change the legal burden of proof — the party who bore it at the start of trial still bears it at the close.
Burden & Standard of Proof.summary_line: BURDEN OF PROOF · CLAIMANT BEARS BURDEN · AFFIRMATIVE DEFENCE · LEGAL v EVIDENTIAL BURDEN · CRA 2015 REVERSAL · s33 LA 1980

Burden & Standard of Proof.issue: Whether there is a single standard of proof in civil proceedings, or whether a higher standard applies to serious allegations.
Burden & Standard of Proof.rule: There is one standard of proof in civil proceedings — the balance of probabilities. There is no intermediate standard and no 'sliding scale' that requires more cogent evidence simply because the allegation is more serious. The HL overruled the earlier approach in Re H (Minors) [1996] AC 563, which had suggested that the more serious the allegation, the stronger the evidence needed to meet the civil standard. In practice, serious and improbable events will require cogent evidence to be proved — but this is because inherently improbable events are less likely to have occurred, not because a higher standard of proof applies.
Burden & Standard of Proof.application: Care proceedings in which serious abuse was alleged. The House of Lords held that the judge had misdirected herself by applying an elevated standard. The question was simply whether, on the balance of probabilities, the events occurred — not whether the evidence was sufficiently compelling for a more serious finding.
Burden & Standard of Proof.conclusion: Settled definitively that civil proceedings apply a single standard of proof — the balance of probabilities — regardless of the gravity of the allegation. This has been consistently applied since. ⚠️ A common exam trap is to apply a higher standard to allegations of fraud or dishonesty in civil proceedings — the standard remains the balance of probabilities. Note the contrast with criminal proceedings (beyond reasonable doubt), and with contempt of court proceedings (criminal standard applies even though they arise in civil proceedings).
Burden & Standard of Proof.summary_line: BALANCE OF PROBABILITIES · SINGLE CIVIL STANDARD · NO SLIDING SCALE · OVERRULES RE H [1996] · COGENT EVIDENCE NEEDED IN PRACTICE · CONTRAST CRIMINAL STANDARD

Burden & Standard of Proof.front: Going deeper: What justifies applying the criminal standard of proof (beyond reasonable doubt) to contempt of court in civil proceedings — and does this produce anomalous results?
Burden & Standard of Proof.answer:
Contempt of court proceedings arising in civil proceedings are classified as civil but require the criminal standard of proof (beyond reasonable doubt): Re Bramblevale Ltd [1970] CA; Dean v Dean [1987]. The justification is that the sanction (committal up to 2 years) is quasi-criminal in nature.

This produces acknowledged anomalies: (1) the civil claimant bringing a contempt application bears a criminal burden but has only civil procedural tools (witness statements, civil disclosure) to establish their case; (2) in defamation proceedings, the underlying claim may be decided on the balance of probabilities, but breach of an injunction in those proceedings requires the criminal standard — a mismatch; (3) in freezing order contexts, a director who dissipates assets in breach may escape committal for want of proof beyond reasonable doubt, even where civil liability is clear.

Professor Lowe and Borrie (Contempt, Sweet & Maxwell) identify a persistent tension in whether contempt should be treated as a protective civil remedy (where the civil standard might apply) or a punitive quasi-criminal process (requiring criminal standards). The Law Commission has not reviewed civil contempt since its 1994 report. The Contempt of Court Act 1981 addressed strict liability contempt for publications but did not harmonise the standard of proof for civil contempt.

⚠️ Not SQE-tested: SQE covers the criminal standard requirement for contempt at the practical level; the jurisprudential rationale is beyond the syllabus.
Burden & Standard of Proof.summary_line: CONTEMPT · CRIMINAL STANDARD · RE BRAMBLEVALE [1970] · CIVIL PROCEDURE + CRIMINAL STANDARD = ANOMALY · LOWE AND BORRIE · LAW COMMISSION 1994

Overview & Methods.front: What are the main methods of enforcing a money judgment?
Overview & Methods.summary_line: ENFORCEMENT · WARRANT OF CONTROL · THIRD PARTY DEBT ORDER · CHARGING ORDER · ATTACHMENT OF EARNINGS · INSOLVENCY

Charging Order.front: How is a charging order obtained and enforced?
Charging Order.answer:
A charging order is obtained in two stages under the Charging Orders Act 1979:
(1) Interim charging order: made on paper without notice. The court considers whether to make any order and, if so, fixes a hearing date.
(2) Final charging order: made at a hearing at which the debtor (and any other interested party) may object. The court considers the personal circumstances of the debtor and any prior incumbrances.

A charging order does not compel payment — it secures the debt against the property. To realise the charge, the creditor must make a separate application for an order for sale. The court exercises a wide discretion on whether to order sale, particularly where the property is the debtor's only or family home and others have an interest in it.
Charging Order.summary_line: CHARGING ORDER · TWO STAGES · INTERIM WITHOUT NOTICE · FINAL HEARING · DOES NOT COMPEL PAYMENT · ORDER FOR SALE SEPARATE APPLICATION

Charging Order.front: After obtaining a judgment for £50,000, the creditor obtains an interim charging order over the debtor's house. The debtor argues the final charging order should not be made because his wife has an equitable interest in the property. Advise.
Charging Order.answer: At the final charging order hearing, the court has wide discretion (Charging Orders Act 1979). The wife has an equitable interest and is entitled to be heard as an interested party. Relevant factors: the value of the debtor's share of the equity; the impact on the wife and any children; the size of the debt; whether other enforcement methods are available. The court may refuse to make the charging order final, or make it but decline to order immediate sale. Even if the final charging order is made, a separate application for an order for sale is required — the charging order alone does not compel sale. Significant weight is given to occupation interests of the family.
Charging Order.summary_line: CHARGING ORDER · FINAL HEARING · WIFE'S EQUITABLE INTEREST · WIDE DISCRETION · IMPACT ON FAMILY HOME · DOES NOT COMPEL SALE · ORDER FOR SALE SEPARATE

Third Party Debt Order.front: How is a third party debt order obtained?
Third Party Debt Order.answer:
A third party debt order (CPR 72) is obtained in two stages:
(1) Interim order: made on paper without notice, freezing the debt owed by the third party to the judgment debtor as at the date of the order.
(2) Final order: made at a hearing at which the third party and judgment debtor may attend and raise objections. If made final, the third party pays the sum directly to the judgment creditor.

The debt must be owed to the judgment debtor (not contingent or future), must be situated within the jurisdiction, and must be immediately payable or payable on demand. Bank and building society accounts are the most common target.

A third party debt order does not give the creditor priority over other creditors in the event of the debtor's insolvency.
Third Party Debt Order.summary_line: THIRD PARTY DEBT ORDER · CPR 72 · INTERIM WITHOUT NOTICE · FREEZES DEBT · FINAL HEARING · BANK ACCOUNTS · NO INSOLVENCY PRIORITY

Contempt & Injunction Enforcement.front: How is a court injunction enforced against a respondent who does not comply, and what is the procedure?
Contempt & Injunction Enforcement.answer:
Non-compliance with a court injunction (or an undertaking to the court) constitutes contempt of court. The remedies available are:
(1) Committal proceedings: an application to commit the respondent to prison for up to 2 years (or to fine or sequestrate assets). The respondent must have had knowledge of the order and deliberately disobeyed it. The standard of proof is the criminal standard — beyond reasonable doubt.
(2) Sequestration: seizure of the respondent's assets pending compliance (used against companies and individuals).

Procedure: the applicant must ensure a penal notice (a formal warning that breach may result in imprisonment) was included in the original order. Personal service of the order is required — service on a solicitor is not sufficient unless expressly authorised. The applicant must also comply with CPR PD 81 on contempt applications, including filing an application notice and a statement of the grounds.

⚠️ The criminal standard of proof applies in contempt proceedings even though they arise in civil proceedings.
Contempt & Injunction Enforcement.summary_line: CONTEMPT OF COURT · NON-COMPLIANCE WITH INJUNCTION · COMMITTAL UP TO 2 YEARS · CRIMINAL STANDARD · PENAL NOTICE · PERSONAL SERVICE · CPR PD 81

Contempt & Injunction Enforcement.front: An injunction orders a company not to continue building work pending trial. The company continues the work. The claimant writes to the company's solicitors. The company argues the injunction was not personally served on its director. Can committal proceedings be brought?
Contempt & Injunction Enforcement.answer: Contempt proceedings for breach of an injunction require: (1) knowledge of the order; and (2) deliberate disobedience. To enforce an injunction against a company personally against its directors, the order (including a penal notice — warning that breach may result in imprisonment) must have been personally served on the relevant director. Service on the company's solicitors is generally insufficient for committal against an individual director unless they were expressly authorised to accept committal applications. If the director was not personally served, committal proceedings cannot currently be brought. The claimant should arrange personal service on the relevant director with the order prominently endorsed with the penal notice.
Contempt & Injunction Enforcement.summary_line: CONTEMPT · BREACH OF INJUNCTION · PERSONAL SERVICE ON DIRECTOR REQUIRED · PENAL NOTICE · SERVICE ON SOLICITORS INSUFFICIENT · RE-SERVE FIRST

Permission & Grounds.front: When is permission to appeal required and on what basis is it granted?
Permission & Grounds.answer:
Permission to appeal is required for virtually all civil appeals (CPR 52.3). It will be granted where: (1) the appeal would have a real prospect of success; or (2) there is some other compelling reason why the appeal should be heard.

Permission may be sought from the lower court at the hearing at which the decision is made, or from the appeal court on paper or at an oral hearing. If the lower court refuses permission, the applicant may renew the application before the appeal court (but not appeal the refusal of permission itself).

⚠️ The 'real prospect of success' test mirrors the summary judgment test — the appeal must have a genuine (not fanciful) prospect of succeeding, but the court does not assess the merits in full at the permission stage.
Permission & Grounds.summary_line: PERMISSION TO APPEAL · CPR 52.3 · REAL PROSPECT OF SUCCESS · COMPELLING REASON · LOWER COURT OR APPEAL COURT · RENEW IF REFUSED

Permission & Grounds.front: On what grounds will an appeal succeed and what approach does the appeal court take?
Permission & Grounds.answer:
An appeal will succeed where the lower court's decision was (CPR 52.21(3)): (1) wrong (i.e. legally erroneous, or a finding of fact that no reasonable judge could have made); or (2) unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity in the proceedings.

The appeal court reviews — it does not rehear — the decision. It considers the lower court's reasoning, not the case afresh. The appeal court is reluctant to interfere with findings of fact made by a judge who heard oral evidence and assessed witness credibility. Errors of law are more readily corrected on appeal.
Permission & Grounds.summary_line: GROUNDS OF APPEAL · WRONG · SERIOUS PROCEDURAL IRREGULARITY · CPR 52.21(3) · REVIEW NOT REHEARING · RELUCTANT ON FACTS

Permission & Grounds.front: The claimant's summary judgment application is refused by a District Judge. The claimant wants to appeal. What are the procedural steps?
Permission & Grounds.answer: The claimant must first obtain permission to appeal. Permission may be sought: (1) from the District Judge at the end of the hearing; or (2) if not sought then or if refused, from the Circuit Judge (the appeal court) on paper or at an oral hearing. The route of appeal from a District Judge is to the Circuit Judge within the County Court. Permission is granted if the appeal has a real prospect of success, or if there is some other compelling reason (CPR 52.3). The appellant's notice must be filed within 21 days of the decision. ⚠️ If the District Judge refuses permission, the claimant may renew before the Circuit Judge — the refusal of permission cannot itself be appealed.
Permission & Grounds.summary_line: PERMISSION TO APPEAL · CPR 52.3 · DISTRICT JUDGE → CIRCUIT JUDGE · SEEK AT HEARING OR FROM APPEAL COURT · REAL PROSPECT · 21 DAYS FOR APPELLANT'S NOTICE · RENEW IF REFUSED

Permission & Grounds.front: Following a High Court trial, the defendant appeals to the Court of Appeal arguing the judge made errors of fact in preferring the claimant's expert over the defendant's expert. Is the appeal likely to succeed?
Permission & Grounds.answer: An appeal succeeds if the lower court's decision was 'wrong' or 'unjust because of a serious procedural or other irregularity' (CPR 52.21(3)). The Court of Appeal reviews, rather than rehearses. It is particularly reluctant to disturb findings of fact by a judge who has heard oral evidence and assessed witness credibility. Preferring one expert's evidence over another's is quintessentially a finding of fact. To succeed, the defendant would need to show either: (a) the judge made an error of law in the assessment; or (b) the finding was so plainly against the weight of the evidence that no reasonable judge could have made it. A bare disagreement with which expert the judge preferred is insufficient.
Permission & Grounds.summary_line: APPEAL · CPR 52.21(3) · REVIEW NOT REHEARING · FINDINGS OF FACT: RELUCTANT TO DISTURB · EXPERT PREFERENCE = FACT · NEED ERROR OF LAW OR PERVERSE FINDING

Routes of Appeal.front: What are the routes of appeal from the County Court and what additional test applies to second appeals?
Routes of Appeal.summary_line: ROUTES OF APPEAL · DISTRICT -> CIRCUIT -> HIGH COURT -> COURT OF APPEAL · SECOND APPEALS CPR 52.7 · IMPORTANT POINT OF PRINCIPLE
